**[Section 1 --- Purpose and Scope]{.underline}**

**[1.1 Purpose]{.underline}**

This Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan (the \"Plan\") establishes the procedures and organizational framework for identifying, containing, eradicating, and recovering from cybersecurity incidents affecting the information technology systems and data assets of Vantage Industrial Technologies, Inc. (the \"Company\"). The Plan provides a structured methodology to ensure that cybersecurity events are addressed in a timely, organized, and effective manner by trained personnel using pre-defined procedures and communication channels.

This Plan is designed to minimize disruption to business operations, protect company and customer data, and ensure timely restoration of affected systems. By establishing clear roles, responsibilities, escalation paths, and response procedures, this Plan aims to reduce the potential impact of cybersecurity incidents on the Company\'s technology infrastructure and to promote a culture of security awareness and operational resilience across the organization. The procedures set forth herein are informed by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) and NIST Special Publication 800-61, Revision 2 (*Computer Security Incident Handling Guide*), and are intended to reflect industry best practices for incident response within an industrial manufacturing environment.

**[1.2 Scope]{.underline}**

This Plan applies to all cybersecurity incidents affecting information technology (IT) systems, networks, applications, and data owned, operated, or managed by Vantage Industrial Technologies, Inc., including systems hosted by third-party service providers on behalf of the Company. The scope of this Plan encompasses the Company\'s entire IT footprint, including but not limited to:

> • Corporate headquarters located at 4200 Commerce Park Drive, Suite 800, Columbus, Ohio 43219;
>
> • All fourteen (14) manufacturing facilities operated by the Company across the United States, Germany, and Mexico;
>
> • Remote access infrastructure, including virtual private network (VPN) gateways and associated authentication systems;
>
> • Cloud-hosted applications and data repositories;
>
> • Enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems and associated modules (finance, procurement, order management);
>
> • Email systems, collaboration platforms, and internal communication tools; and
>
> • Approximately 2,100 networked endpoints across the enterprise, including workstations, servers, laptops, and mobile devices enrolled in the Company\'s endpoint management platform.

This Plan addresses incidents affecting the Company\'s Information Technology (IT) environment. Operational Technology (OT) and SCADA systems are governed by separate operational safety procedures and are outside the scope of this Plan. The Company maintains the IT and OT networks as air-gapped environments to reduce the risk of cross-domain compromise. Any incident that is suspected of affecting OT or SCADA systems should be immediately reported to the Director of Manufacturing Operations in addition to the Incident Commander, and the applicable OT safety procedures shall take precedence in such scenarios.

**[1.3 Applicability]{.underline}**

This Plan applies to all employees, contractors, temporary workers, and third-party vendors who have been granted access to Vantage Industrial Technologies, Inc.\'s IT systems, networks, or data. As of the effective date of this Plan, the Company employs approximately 6,800 individuals across its corporate and manufacturing operations. In addition, the Company maintains VPN-credentialed access for authorized third-party maintenance contractors who provide remote and on-site technical support for various IT and manufacturing support systems. All persons within the scope of this Plan are expected to comply with the Company\'s information security policies and to promptly report any suspected cybersecurity incidents to the Security Operations Center (SOC) or their immediate supervisor.

**[1.4 Document Governance]{.underline}**

This Plan shall be reviewed annually by the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) and updated as necessary to reflect changes in the Company\'s technology environment, threat landscape, organizational structure, or operational requirements. Following any substantive revision, the Plan shall be submitted to the Chief Technology Officer (CTO) for re-approval prior to re-issuance.

The most recent substantive review was conducted in June 2022. An administrative review was completed on August 14, 2023, updating vendor contact information in Appendix C. No changes were made to the Plan\'s procedures, governance structure, classification framework, or communication protocols during the August 2023 review.

No tabletop exercises or simulation-based tests of this Plan have been conducted. The CISO shall develop an exercise schedule and ensure that the Plan is tested through periodic simulations. The version history of this Plan is maintained at the end of this document.

All questions regarding this Plan should be directed to the CISO. The CISO shall maintain the master copy of this Plan and is responsible for distributing updated versions to all IRT members and other designated personnel.

**[Section 2 --- Governance and Organizational Authority]{.underline}**

**[2.1 Plan Ownership]{.underline}**

The Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), currently Derek Fong, is the owner of this Plan and is responsible for its maintenance, periodic review, updates, and activation. The CISO reports to the Chief Technology Officer (CTO), currently Lauren Hessler, within the Company\'s organizational structure. The CISO shall coordinate with the Information Technology, Information Security, Facilities, and Human Resources departments in executing this Plan. The CISO shall ensure that all IRT members have access to the current version of this Plan and are familiar with their roles and responsibilities as described herein.

**[2.2 Incident Commander]{.underline}**

The CISO serves as the \"Incident Commander\" for all cybersecurity incidents governed by this Plan. The Incident Commander is the central authority responsible for directing the Company\'s response to any cybersecurity incident from initial detection through final closure. The Incident Commander position is critical to maintaining a unified, coordinated response effort and ensuring that all response activities are properly documented and executed in accordance with established procedures.

The Incident Commander has the authority to:

> \(a\) Activate the Incident Response Team (IRT) and convene IRT members for coordinated response activities;
>
> \(b\) Declare and assign incident severity levels in accordance with the classification criteria set forth in Section 3.2 of this Plan;
>
> \(c\) Authorize containment actions, including but not limited to system isolation, network segmentation, credential revocation, and emergency firewall rule implementation;
>
> \(d\) Engage pre-approved third-party forensic and remediation vendors under existing retainer agreements, as listed in Appendix C of this Plan;
>
> \(e\) Authorize emergency IT expenditures up to a cumulative total of \$250,000 per incident without requiring additional management approval, provided that such expenditures are documented in the ServiceNow incident record and reported to the CTO within forty-eight (48) hours; and
>
> \(f\) Determine when the incident has been fully resolved and authorize closure of the incident record in ServiceNow.

The Incident Commander shall exercise sole authority over incident classification, response coordination, and closure determination. In the event that the CISO is unavailable, the Director of IT Infrastructure shall serve as the alternate Incident Commander until the CISO is available to resume command. The Incident Commander may delegate specific tasks to IRT members but retains overall accountability for the response effort.

**[2.3 Incident Response Team (IRT) Composition]{.underline}**

The Incident Response Team (IRT) is a standing cross-functional team responsible for executing the Company\'s response to cybersecurity incidents under the direction of the Incident Commander. The IRT is composed of the following standing members:

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Role**                     **Department**           **Named Member**
  ---------------------------- ------------------------ ---------------------------------
  Incident Commander (Chair)   Information Security     Derek Fong, CISO

  IT Infrastructure Lead       Information Technology   Director of IT Infrastructure

  Network Security Analyst     Information Security     Senior Network Security Analyst

  Endpoint Security Analyst    Information Security     Endpoint Security Engineer

  Facilities Coordinator       Facilities Management    Director of Facilities

  HR Representative            Human Resources          VP of Human Resources
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The IRT is a standing cross-functional team with the members listed above. Additional personnel may be consulted on an as-needed basis at the discretion of the Incident Commander. The Incident Commander may request temporary support from other Company departments or external parties as circumstances require, and such requests shall be documented in the ServiceNow incident record.

Each IRT member is expected to be available for activation within one (1) hour of notification during normal business hours and within two (2) hours during non-business hours. IRT members shall maintain current contact information on file with the CISO and shall notify the CISO promptly of any changes to their availability, including planned vacations or leaves of absence. Each IRT role has a designated alternate who may serve in the event that the primary member is unavailable; alternates are identified by the respective department heads and maintained on file by the CISO.

The IRT convenes upon activation by the Incident Commander for any Tier 2 (Medium) or Tier 3 (High) incident, as described in Section 3.2 of this Plan. For Tier 1 (Low) incidents, the SOC team may address the incident without full IRT activation, subject to post-resolution review by the Incident Commander.

**[2.4 Roles and Responsibilities]{.underline}**

The following describes the specific roles and responsibilities of each standing IRT member during an active cybersecurity incident.

**Incident Commander (CISO)**

The Incident Commander serves as the overall coordinator and decision-maker for the incident response effort. Specific responsibilities include:

> • Directing all IRT activities and ensuring adherence to this Plan;
>
> • Performing initial and ongoing severity classification in accordance with the criteria set forth in Section 3.2;
>
> • Coordinating communications among IRT members and within the IT and Information Security departments;
>
> • Authorizing and directing all containment, eradication, and recovery actions;
>
> • Engaging and managing pre-approved third-party forensic and remediation vendors;
>
> • Authorizing emergency IT expenditures within approved limits;
>
> • Maintaining oversight of all incident documentation in ServiceNow;
>
> • Determining when the incident is resolved and authorizing closure of the incident record;
>
> • Preparing or overseeing preparation of the post-incident report in accordance with Section 8.1; and
>
> • Reporting incident status and post-incident findings to the CTO.

**IT Infrastructure Lead (Director of IT Infrastructure)**

The IT Infrastructure Lead is responsible for all actions related to the Company\'s core IT infrastructure during an incident. Specific responsibilities include:

> • Executing system isolation and network segmentation actions as directed by the Incident Commander;
>
> • Coordinating backup restoration activities for affected systems, prioritizing Tier 1 (critical) systems;
>
> • Managing infrastructure capacity and performance during incident response to minimize impact on unaffected operations;
>
> • Overseeing the rebuilding and redeployment of compromised servers, workstations, and network components;
>
> • Coordinating with data center operations and cloud service providers as necessary;
>
> • Providing status updates to the Incident Commander on infrastructure recovery progress; and
>
> • Documenting all infrastructure-related actions and timelines in ServiceNow.

**Network Security Analyst (Senior Network Security Analyst)**

The Network Security Analyst is responsible for network-layer analysis and defense during an incident. Specific responsibilities include:

> • Analyzing network traffic patterns to identify indicators of compromise (IOCs) and lateral movement;
>
> • Implementing emergency firewall rule changes and access control list modifications as directed by the Incident Commander;
>
> • Reviewing intrusion detection system (IDS) and intrusion prevention system (IPS) alerts and logs;
>
> • Correlating threat intelligence feeds with observed network activity to identify threat actor tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs);
>
> • Performing packet capture and analysis as needed for forensic purposes;
>
> • Monitoring network perimeter defenses for evidence of continued or renewed adversary activity; and
>
> • Documenting all network-related findings and actions in ServiceNow.

**Endpoint Security Analyst (Endpoint Security Engineer)**

The Endpoint Security Analyst is responsible for endpoint-layer analysis and remediation. Specific responsibilities include:

> • Performing malware analysis on samples recovered from affected endpoints;
>
> • Executing endpoint isolation procedures using the Company\'s endpoint detection and response (EDR) platform;
>
> • Capturing and preserving forensic disk images of compromised endpoints for technical analysis;
>
> • Deploying updated antivirus signatures, EDR detection rules, and endpoint hardening configurations;
>
> • Conducting endpoint scanning across the enterprise to identify additional compromised systems;
>
> • Coordinating with the third-party forensics vendor for advanced malware reverse engineering; and
>
> • Documenting all endpoint-related findings and actions in ServiceNow.

**Facilities Coordinator (Director of Facilities)**

The Facilities Coordinator is responsible for physical security measures that may be required during an incident. Specific responsibilities include:

> • Implementing emergency physical access control changes, including badge access revocation or modification for affected areas;
>
> • Coordinating physical isolation of server rooms, data closets, or other facilities housing affected equipment;
>
> • Liaising with building management and physical security contractors as necessary;
>
> • Providing on-site support for equipment inspection, relocation, or decommissioning; and
>
> • Documenting all facilities-related actions in ServiceNow.

**HR Representative (VP of Human Resources)**

The HR Representative is responsible for personnel-related matters arising from cybersecurity incidents. Specific responsibilities include:

> • Coordinating responses to insider threat situations, including employee investigations and disciplinary actions;
>
> • Facilitating revocation of system access for terminated or suspended employees or contractors;
>
> • Coordinating communications to affected employees regarding incident-related impacts to their work environment or personal data;
>
> • Advising the Incident Commander on workforce implications of containment and recovery actions; and
>
> • Documenting all HR-related actions and decisions in ServiceNow.

**[Section 3 --- Incident Classification and Severity Levels]{.underline}**

**[3.1 Definition of a Cybersecurity Incident]{.underline}**

For purposes of this Plan, a cybersecurity incident is defined as any event that actually or potentially jeopardizes the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the Company\'s information systems or data, or that constitutes a violation of the Company\'s information security policies. This definition encompasses a broad range of events, including but not limited to unauthorized access to systems or data, malware infections, ransomware attacks, denial-of-service attacks, unauthorized data exfiltration, phishing campaigns that result in credential compromise, exploitation of system vulnerabilities, and insider threats involving misuse of authorized access privileges. An event need not result in confirmed harm to qualify as an incident; suspected or attempted compromises that warrant investigation fall within this definition.

**[3.2 Severity Classification Matrix]{.underline}**

All cybersecurity incidents shall be classified according to the following three-tier severity framework. The Incident Commander shall assign the initial severity classification based on the information available at the time of IRT activation and may reclassify the incident as additional information becomes available, in accordance with Section 3.3.

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Severity Level**      **Classification**     **Criteria**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               **Response Timeframe**
  ----------------------- ---------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Tier 1 --- Low**      Minor Incident         Isolated malware detection on fewer than 10 endpoints; no data exfiltration suspected; no critical system downtime; phishing attempts with no confirmed compromise; unauthorized access attempts that were blocked by existing controls                                                                                                                    Respond within 24 hours of detection; target resolution within 72 hours

  **Tier 2 --- Medium**   Significant Incident   Compromise of 10--100 endpoints; suspected unauthorized access to non-critical systems; limited system downtime of less than 4 hours; detection of unauthorized network reconnaissance or scanning activity; successful phishing resulting in limited credential compromise                                                                                Respond within 4 hours of detection; full IRT activation; target resolution within 48 hours

  **Tier 3 --- High**     Critical Incident      Compromise of more than 100 endpoints OR loss of critical system availability for more than 4 hours; confirmed data exfiltration; ransomware deployment affecting production systems; compromise of privileged credentials (administrator, domain controller); breach of network perimeter defenses; confirmed advanced persistent threat (APT) activity   Respond immediately upon detection; full IRT activation; resolve as rapidly as possible
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Severity classification is determined by the Incident Commander based solely on the technical criteria set forth above. The criteria are designed to reflect the scope and nature of the technical compromise and its impact on the Company\'s IT infrastructure. The number of affected endpoints, duration of system downtime, and nature of the technical attack vector are the primary factors in determining the appropriate severity tier.

When an incident involves characteristics spanning multiple severity tiers, the Incident Commander shall classify the incident at the highest applicable tier. For example, an incident involving fewer than 10 endpoints but with confirmed data exfiltration shall be classified as Tier 3 (High) because confirmed data exfiltration is a Tier 3 criterion regardless of endpoint count.

The response timeframes specified above represent target response and resolution windows. Actual response and resolution times may vary depending on the complexity and scope of the incident. All response timeframe milestones shall be documented in the ServiceNow incident record.

**[3.3 Reclassification]{.underline}**

The Incident Commander may reclassify an incident\'s severity level at any time as new technical information becomes available during the course of the investigation and response. Reclassification decisions shall be based on updated assessments of the technical criteria set forth in Section 3.2, including revised endpoint counts, newly identified data compromise, extended system downtime, or discovery of additional attack vectors. All reclassification decisions and the technical basis therefor shall be documented in the incident record maintained in ServiceNow, including the date and time of reclassification and a brief summary of the new information that prompted the change.

**[Section 4 --- Phase 1: Preparation]{.underline}**

**[4.1 Preventive Controls and Readiness]{.underline}**

The Company maintains the following standing security controls and readiness measures to reduce the likelihood and potential impact of cybersecurity incidents and to ensure that the IRT is prepared to respond effectively when incidents occur:

**(a) Asset Inventory.** The Company maintains an up-to-date inventory of all networked assets, including servers, workstations, laptops, mobile devices, network equipment, and IoT devices. As of the most recent inventory cycle, the Company manages approximately 2,100 networked endpoints across its corporate and manufacturing IT environments. The asset inventory is maintained in the Company\'s IT asset management (ITAM) system and is updated on a continuous basis as new devices are deployed or decommissioned. Accurate asset inventory is essential for rapid identification of affected systems during an incident.

**(b) Network Segmentation.** The Company maintains logical and physical segmentation between its IT and OT network environments. The IT and OT networks are air-gapped to prevent cross-domain compromise. Within the IT environment, network segmentation is implemented through VLANs, firewall zones, and access control lists to limit lateral movement in the event of a compromise. Critical systems, including ERP modules, are hosted on dedicated network segments with restricted access.

**(c) Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR).** The Company deploys an enterprise EDR platform across all managed endpoints. The EDR platform provides real-time monitoring, behavioral analysis, automated threat detection, and remote response capabilities. EDR telemetry is ingested into the Company\'s security information and event management (SIEM) platform for centralized correlation and alerting.

**(d) Remote Access Security.** Remote access for employees and authorized third-party contractors is provided exclusively through the Company\'s VPN infrastructure. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is required for all VPN connections. VPN access is provisioned through a formal request and approval process, and VPN credentials are reviewed quarterly.

**(e) Vulnerability Management.** The Company conducts quarterly vulnerability scanning of all internet-facing and internal systems using automated scanning tools. Annual penetration testing is performed by a qualified third-party vendor to identify and validate exploitable vulnerabilities. Findings from vulnerability scans and penetration tests are tracked through remediation to closure.

**(f) Security Awareness Training.** All Company employees are required to complete security awareness training annually. Training covers topics including phishing recognition, password hygiene, social engineering tactics, acceptable use of Company systems, and incident reporting procedures. Training completion rates are tracked and reported to the CISO.

**(g) Third-Party Forensics Retainer.** The Company maintains a pre-negotiated retainer agreement with Thorngate Forensic Solutions, Inc. for digital forensics and incident response (DFIR) services. The retainer ensures that qualified forensic investigators are available for rapid engagement in the event of a significant or critical incident. Additional approved vendors are listed in Appendix C.

**[4.2 Backup and Recovery Readiness]{.underline}**

The Company maintains a comprehensive backup and recovery strategy to support rapid restoration of affected systems following a cybersecurity incident. The backup strategy includes the following key components:

**Backup Schedule.** Daily incremental backups are performed for all critical systems, including ERP modules (finance, procurement, and order management), email systems, file servers, and collaboration platforms. Weekly full backups are performed for all systems in scope. Backup data is encrypted in transit and at rest using AES-256 encryption.

**Offsite Replication.** All backup data is replicated to a geographically separated secondary data center to protect against site-level disasters, including physical destruction of the primary data center. Replication is performed over dedicated, encrypted network links.

**Backup Integrity Testing.** Monthly backup integrity tests are conducted to verify that backup data is complete, uncorrupted, and restorable. A random sample of systems is selected each month for test restoration, and results are documented and reviewed by the IT Infrastructure Lead.

**Recovery Time Objectives (RTO).** The Company has established the following recovery time objectives for its IT systems:

> • **Tier 1 Systems (ERP --- finance, procurement, order management):** Maximum RTO of 24 hours. These systems are essential to core business operations and are prioritized for restoration.
>
> • **Tier 2 Systems (all other production systems):** Maximum RTO of 72 hours.

**Recovery Point Objective (RPO).** The maximum acceptable data loss for critical systems is 4 hours. This is supported by the daily incremental backup schedule and transaction log backups for database systems, which are performed at four-hour intervals.

The IT Infrastructure Lead is responsible for maintaining the backup and recovery infrastructure and for coordinating restoration activities during an incident under the direction of the Incident Commander.

**[4.3 Training and Exercises]{.underline}**

All IRT members shall receive incident response training upon joining the team and annually thereafter. Training shall cover the contents of this Plan, including incident classification procedures, escalation protocols, evidence collection practices, containment and eradication techniques, and the use of the ServiceNow incident management platform. Training may be delivered through a combination of instructor-led sessions, online modules, and self-study materials, as determined by the CISO.

The CISO shall schedule periodic tabletop exercises to test the Plan\'s effectiveness and identify areas for improvement. Tabletop exercises simulate realistic cybersecurity incident scenarios and require IRT members to walk through the response procedures outlined in this Plan in a facilitated discussion format. The results of each tabletop exercise shall be documented, including identified gaps, areas for improvement, and recommended updates to this Plan or to the Company\'s security controls.

As of the date of this Plan, no tabletop exercises have been scheduled. The CISO will develop an exercise schedule following initial Plan deployment. Training records are maintained by the Information Security department.

**[Section 5 --- Phase 2: Detection and Analysis]{.underline}**

**[5.1 Detection Sources]{.underline}**

Cybersecurity incidents may be detected through any of the following channels:

**(a) Security Operations Center (SOC) Monitoring.** The Company\'s SOC operates on a 24/7 basis and serves as the primary detection and monitoring function. SOC analysts continuously monitor security alerts generated by the SIEM platform, EDR tools, intrusion detection systems, and other security technologies.

**(b) Automated Alerts.** Automated alerts from the Company\'s EDR platform, SIEM, intrusion detection and prevention systems (IDS/IPS), data loss prevention (DLP) tools, and email security gateway may trigger incident investigation.

**(c) Employee and Contractor Reports.** Employees and contractors are encouraged to report suspected security incidents, suspicious emails, unusual system behavior, or other potential indicators of compromise to the SOC via email (soc@vantageindustrial.com) or by calling the SOC hotline.

**(d) Third-Party Vendor or Partner Notifications.** Notifications received from third-party service providers, technology vendors, or business partners regarding vulnerabilities, compromises, or suspicious activity affecting systems connected to or shared with the Company.

**(e) Law Enforcement Notifications.** Notifications received from federal, state, or local law enforcement agencies regarding threats or compromises affecting the Company or its industry sector.

**(f) Customer Reports.** Reports received from customers regarding data irregularities, suspicious communications purporting to be from the Company, or other indicators suggesting a potential compromise.

**[5.2 Initial Triage and Analysis]{.underline}**

Upon detection of a potential cybersecurity incident through any of the channels described in Section 5.1, the following initial triage and analysis procedures shall be followed:

**(a) Incident Ticket Creation.** The SOC analyst who identifies or receives the report shall immediately create an incident ticket in the ServiceNow incident management platform. The ticket shall include all available initial details, including the date and time of detection, the detection source, a preliminary description of the event, and the identity of the reporting individual or system.

**(b) Preliminary Scope Assessment.** The SOC analyst shall perform a preliminary analysis to determine the scope of the event, including identification of affected systems, endpoints, network segments, and user accounts. The analyst shall leverage available telemetry from the SIEM, EDR, and other security tools to characterize the event.

**(c) Incident Determination.** The SOC analyst shall assess whether the event meets the definition of a cybersecurity incident as set forth in Section 3.1 of this Plan. Events that do not meet the incident definition shall be documented and closed as false positives or security events (non-incidents) in ServiceNow.

**(d) Preliminary Severity Classification.** For events confirmed or suspected to be cybersecurity incidents, the SOC analyst shall assign a preliminary severity level in accordance with the classification matrix in Section 3.2, based on available information.

**(e) Escalation to Incident Commander.** For incidents classified as Tier 2 (Medium) or Tier 3 (High), the SOC analyst shall immediately notify the Incident Commander (CISO) by phone and email. For Tier 1 (Low) incidents, the SOC team may handle investigation and resolution without full IRT activation, subject to post-resolution review and sign-off by the Incident Commander. All Tier 1 incident closures shall be reviewed by the Incident Commander within five (5) business days of closure.

**[5.3 Evidence Collection]{.underline}**

The IRT shall collect and preserve technical evidence relevant to the incident for purposes of technical analysis, root cause determination, and remediation validation. Evidence collection is a critical component of the incident response process and shall be conducted with care to ensure accuracy and completeness.

Technical evidence to be collected and preserved includes, but is not limited to:

> • System logs (operating system, application, authentication, and security logs) from affected and potentially affected systems;
>
> • Network traffic captures (full packet capture and NetFlow data) from relevant network segments;
>
> • Malware samples recovered from affected endpoints, stored in a secure quarantine environment;
>
> • Forensic disk images of compromised endpoints, created using industry-standard forensic imaging tools;
>
> • Screenshots of anomalous system behavior, error messages, or indicators of compromise;
>
> • Firewall, IDS/IPS, and proxy logs for the relevant timeframe;
>
> • Email headers and message content associated with phishing or social engineering vectors; and
>
> • EDR telemetry and behavioral analysis data from the endpoint detection platform.

All evidence collected during the course of an incident shall be documented in the ServiceNow incident record with timestamps and the identity of the collecting analyst. Each evidence item shall be tagged with a unique identifier and stored in a designated secure evidence repository maintained by the Information Security department. The Endpoint Security Analyst is the primary responsible party for forensic image creation, and the Network Security Analyst is the primary responsible party for network traffic capture. The Incident Commander shall oversee evidence collection activities and ensure that all relevant evidence is captured in a timely manner.

Evidence shall be retained for a minimum of twelve (12) months following incident closure, or longer if required by ongoing technical investigation. The retention period may be extended at the discretion of the Incident Commander.

**[Section 6 --- Phase 3: Containment]{.underline}**

**[6.1 Short-Term Containment]{.underline}**

Upon classification and IRT activation, the first priority of the incident response is to contain the incident to prevent further spread or damage. Short-term containment measures are intended to be executed rapidly to limit the immediate impact of the incident while preserving evidence for further analysis. The Incident Commander shall authorize all containment actions.

Short-term containment actions may include, but are not limited to:

**(a) Endpoint Isolation.** Affected endpoints shall be isolated from the corporate network using the EDR platform\'s remote isolation capability or by physical disconnection. Isolation prevents lateral movement from compromised systems to unaffected areas of the network.

**(b) Credential Revocation.** User accounts and service accounts that are confirmed or suspected to be compromised shall be immediately disabled or have their passwords reset. This includes VPN credentials, domain accounts, application accounts, and any privileged or administrative credentials associated with affected systems.

**(c) Emergency Firewall Rules.** The Network Security Analyst shall implement emergency firewall rules and access control list modifications to block known malicious IP addresses, domains, and command-and-control (C2) infrastructure associated with the incident. These rules shall be implemented at the network perimeter and at internal segmentation boundaries as appropriate.

**(d) Forensic Preservation.** Prior to any remediation activities that would alter the state of affected systems, the Endpoint Security Analyst shall create forensic disk images of compromised endpoints. Forensic images shall be stored in the secure evidence repository and documented in ServiceNow. This step ensures that technical evidence is available for subsequent analysis and root cause determination.

All short-term containment actions shall be documented in real time in the ServiceNow incident record, including the action taken, the systems and accounts affected, the IRT member who executed the action, and the date and time of execution.

**[6.2 Long-Term Containment]{.underline}**

Following initial short-term containment, the IRT shall implement long-term containment measures designed to maintain business operations while eradication and recovery efforts are prepared and executed. Long-term containment recognizes that complete eradication may require time and planning, and that the Company may need to operate in a partially compromised or restricted state during this period.

Long-term containment actions may include, but are not limited to:

**(a) Temporary Workarounds.** The IT Infrastructure Lead shall coordinate the deployment of temporary workarounds for affected business processes, such as alternative system access methods, manual processes for critical workflows, or temporary server deployments to maintain essential services.

**(b) Enhanced Monitoring.** The Network Security Analyst and Endpoint Security Analyst shall implement enhanced monitoring on unaffected systems and network segments to detect any expansion of the incident. This may include additional SIEM correlation rules, increased EDR scan frequency, and targeted network traffic analysis.

**(c) Third-Party Forensics Engagement.** The Incident Commander shall engage Thorngate Forensic Solutions, Inc. (or another pre-approved forensics vendor listed in Appendix C) for deep-dive forensic analysis if the incident is classified as Tier 2 (Medium) or Tier 3 (High) and the scope or complexity exceeds the IRT\'s internal capabilities. The forensics vendor shall work under the direction of the Incident Commander and report findings directly to the IRT.

**(d) Backup Viability Assessment.** The IT Infrastructure Lead shall assess the viability of backup restoration for affected systems, including verification that backup data predates the initial compromise and has not been corrupted or tampered with by the threat actor. The results of this assessment shall inform the recovery strategy developed under Section 7.

All long-term containment decisions are made by the Incident Commander in coordination with the IRT members. Containment actions shall be documented in ServiceNow and reviewed during daily IRT status meetings for the duration of the active incident.

**[Section 7 --- Phase 4: Eradication and Recovery]{.underline}**

**[7.1 Eradication]{.underline}**

Once containment is established, the IRT shall proceed to eradicate the threat from the Company\'s environment. Eradication involves the complete removal of the threat actor\'s presence, tools, and access from all affected systems and the remediation of the vulnerabilities or weaknesses that were exploited to gain initial access. Eradication actions may include, but are not limited to:

**(a) Malware Removal.** The Endpoint Security Analyst shall identify and remove all malware, backdoors, web shells, remote access tools, and other malicious software from affected endpoints. Removal shall be validated through comprehensive scanning using the EDR platform and additional manual analysis as appropriate.

**(b) Vulnerability Remediation.** The IRT shall identify the vulnerability or attack vector that was exploited to initiate the compromise and shall implement appropriate patches, configuration changes, or other remediation measures to close the identified vulnerability. The Network Security Analyst and Endpoint Security Analyst shall coordinate with the IT Infrastructure Lead on patch deployment.

**(c) Credential Reset.** All credentials that were confirmed or potentially compromised during the incident shall be permanently reset. This includes domain administrator accounts, service accounts, VPN credentials for third-party maintenance contractors, application-specific credentials, and any shared or default credentials that were in use on affected systems. The scope of credential reset shall be determined by the Incident Commander based on the forensic analysis.

**(d) Eradication Validation.** Thorngate Forensic Solutions, Inc. (or another retained forensics vendor) shall validate the completeness of eradication activities. Validation shall include comprehensive scanning of affected and adjacent systems, review of persistence mechanisms (registry keys, scheduled tasks, startup items, rootkits), and verification that all identified indicators of compromise have been remediated.

All eradication actions and validation results shall be documented in the ServiceNow incident record.

**[7.2 System Recovery]{.underline}**

Following successful eradication, the IRT shall proceed to restore affected systems to normal operational status. Recovery is coordinated by the IT Infrastructure Lead under the overall direction of the Incident Commander. The recovery process shall prioritize systems based on the Company\'s established recovery time objectives (RTOs) as described in Section 4.2, with Tier 1 systems (ERP modules for finance, procurement, and order management) receiving highest priority.

Recovery actions shall include, but are not limited to:

**(a) Backup Restoration.** Affected systems shall be restored from verified clean backups that have been confirmed to predate the initial compromise. The IT Infrastructure Lead shall coordinate backup restoration activities with the data center operations team and verify the integrity of restored data.

**(b) System Rebuild.** Endpoints or servers that cannot be reliably restored from backup shall be rebuilt from baseline images and reconfigured to current standards. Rebuilt systems shall be fully patched and hardened before reintroduction to the production network.

**(c) Data Integrity Validation.** Following restoration or rebuild, the IT Infrastructure Lead shall conduct data integrity checks to confirm that restored data is complete, accurate, and uncorrupted. This is particularly critical for ERP database systems and financial data repositories.

**(d) Staged Reintroduction.** Restored and rebuilt systems shall be gradually reintroduced to the production network with enhanced monitoring in place. The Network Security Analyst shall monitor network traffic to and from restored systems for a minimum of seventy-two (72) hours following reintroduction to detect any residual threat activity.

**(e) Recovery Documentation.** All recovery steps, timelines, and milestones shall be documented in the ServiceNow incident record. Deviations from planned recovery timelines shall be noted with an explanation and communicated to the Incident Commander.

**[7.3 Recovery Verification]{.underline}**

Before returning systems to full production operations and reducing enhanced monitoring levels, the Incident Commander shall conduct a formal recovery verification review. This review shall confirm that:

**(a)** All affected systems have been restored to full operational capability and are performing within normal parameters;

**(b)** Comprehensive security validation scans have been completed on all restored and rebuilt systems, with no indicators of residual compromise;

**(c)** Backup integrity has been verified for all restored systems, confirming that ongoing backup processes are functioning correctly;

**(d)** No persistence mechanisms, backdoors, or residual malicious artifacts remain on any system within the affected scope; and

**(e)** All exploited vulnerabilities have been remediated and validated through scanning or testing.

The Incident Commander shall declare the incident resolved when all affected systems have been restored and verified in accordance with the criteria above. The declaration of incident resolution, including the date and time, shall be documented in the ServiceNow incident record. Upon declaration of resolution, enhanced monitoring may be reduced to normal levels at the discretion of the Incident Commander, though the Incident Commander may elect to maintain elevated monitoring for an additional period as a precautionary measure.

**[Section 8 --- Phase 5: Post-Incident Review]{.underline}**

**[8.1 Post-Incident Report]{.underline}**

Within thirty (30) calendar days of incident closure, the Incident Commander shall prepare a comprehensive written post-incident report. The purpose of the post-incident report is to document the complete lifecycle of the incident, capture lessons learned, and identify opportunities for improvement in the Company\'s security posture and incident response capabilities. The post-incident report shall cover the following areas:

**(a) Incident Timeline.** A detailed chronological account of the incident from initial detection through final resolution, including all key milestones, decisions, and actions taken by the IRT.

**(b) Root Cause Analysis.** An analysis of the root cause or causes of the incident, including the initial attack vector, vulnerabilities exploited, and contributing factors that enabled or exacerbated the compromise.

**(c) Scope of Impact.** A comprehensive assessment of the scope of the incident, including the number and identity of affected systems, endpoints, network segments, data repositories, and user accounts. This section shall also describe the nature and extent of any data compromise, including the types of data affected.

**(d) Containment and Eradication Actions.** A summary of all containment and eradication actions taken, including the effectiveness of each action and any challenges encountered during execution.

**(e) Recovery Steps and Timelines.** A summary of all recovery actions, including actual recovery times relative to established RTOs, data integrity validation results, and any deviations from planned recovery procedures.

**(f) Lessons Learned.** An assessment of what worked well during the response, what could be improved, and what gaps or deficiencies were identified in the Company\'s security controls, detection capabilities, response procedures, or this Plan.

**(g) Recommended Improvements.** Specific, actionable recommendations for improving the Company\'s security controls, incident response processes, detection capabilities, training programs, or this Plan based on the lessons learned from the incident.

The completed post-incident report shall be documented in ServiceNow and provided to the Chief Technology Officer (CTO). The Incident Commander shall schedule a post-incident review meeting with all IRT members within fifteen (15) business days of incident closure to discuss findings and recommendations. Action items arising from the post-incident review shall be tracked in ServiceNow and assigned to responsible parties with target completion dates.

**[8.2 Continuous Improvement]{.underline}**

Lessons learned from each incident and post-incident review shall be incorporated into updates to this Plan, security controls, training programs, and detection capabilities as appropriate. The CISO shall track all remediation actions and improvement initiatives arising from post-incident reviews in ServiceNow and shall report on completion status during regular IT security reviews with the CTO. The continuous improvement process is an ongoing cycle designed to strengthen the Company\'s overall cybersecurity posture and incident response readiness over time.

The CISO shall maintain a consolidated log of all post-incident recommendations and their implementation status. This log shall be reviewed during the annual Plan review process described in Section 1.4 to ensure that identified improvements are being addressed in a timely manner.

**[Section 9 --- Communications]{.underline}**

**[9.1 Internal IT Communications]{.underline}**

During an active cybersecurity incident, the Incident Commander shall coordinate all communications within the Information Technology and Information Security departments. To ensure clear, secure, and documented communication among IRT members and supporting personnel, the following communication channels are authorized for incident-related discussions:

**(a) Dedicated Incident Response Slack Channel.** A dedicated Slack channel (#incident-response) shall be used for real-time communication among IRT members during an active incident. Access to this channel is restricted to IRT members and individuals expressly authorized by the Incident Commander.

**(b) ServiceNow Incident Ticket.** The ServiceNow incident ticket serves as the primary record of all incident-related actions, decisions, findings, and communications. All significant actions, status updates, and decisions shall be documented in the ServiceNow ticket in real time.

**(c) Bridge Call / Video Conference.** The Incident Commander shall establish a standing bridge call or video conference for IRT members to facilitate real-time verbal coordination. Bridge call details shall be communicated via the #incident-response Slack channel.

All IRT members must refrain from discussing incident details outside of authorized channels. No information about an active incident shall be communicated outside the IRT without the express authorization of the Incident Commander. This restriction applies to all forms of communication, including email, messaging applications, phone calls, and in-person conversations.

**[9.2 Vendor and Third-Party Communications]{.underline}**

The Incident Commander is authorized to communicate with the following external parties during an active incident, as necessary to support the response effort:

**(a) Retained Forensics Vendor.** Thorngate Forensic Solutions, Inc. (or another pre-approved forensics vendor listed in Appendix C) may be engaged by the Incident Commander for forensic investigation, malware analysis, and eradication support.

**(b) Affected Technology Vendors.** The Incident Commander may communicate with vendors whose products or services are implicated in the incident (e.g., operating system vendors, application vendors, cloud service providers) to obtain patches, threat intelligence, or technical support.

**(c) Managed Security Service Providers.** The Incident Commander may coordinate with the Company\'s managed security service providers as needed to enhance monitoring, adjust detection rules, or obtain additional analyst support.

**(d) Law Enforcement.** The Incident Commander may engage law enforcement agencies, including the FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3), when criminal activity is suspected. Engagement of law enforcement is at the discretion of the Incident Commander and shall be coordinated carefully to avoid interference with the Company\'s investigation and recovery efforts. There is no mandatory requirement under this Plan to involve law enforcement.

All vendor and third-party communications shall be documented in the ServiceNow incident record, including the date and time of communication, the parties involved, the subject matter discussed, and any commitments or action items.

**[9.3 External Communications]{.underline}**

External communications regarding cybersecurity incidents --- including communications to customers, regulators, media, or the public --- are outside the scope of this Plan. The procedures set forth in this Plan are focused on the technical detection, containment, eradication, and recovery lifecycle, and do not govern communications with external stakeholders beyond the technology vendors and forensics providers described in Section 9.2.

If external communications are deemed necessary by the Incident Commander, the Incident Commander should coordinate with the Company\'s corporate communications team to develop appropriate messaging. The corporate communications team is responsible for managing the Company\'s public communications and media relations functions and should be consulted before any incident-related information is shared with external parties.

The Incident Commander shall use professional judgment in determining whether and when to involve the corporate communications team, taking into consideration the scope and severity of the incident and the potential for public awareness.

**[Section 10 --- Escalation Matrix]{.underline}**

**[10.1 Escalation Thresholds]{.underline}**

The following escalation matrix defines the individuals who shall be notified and the timeframes for notification at each incident severity tier. Escalation ensures that appropriate levels of management are informed of cybersecurity incidents and can provide support and guidance to the IRT.

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Severity Level**      **Escalated To**              **Notification Timeframe**                                                                     **Notification Method**
  ----------------------- ----------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------
  **Tier 1 --- Low**      CISO (for awareness only)     Within 24 hours of initial detection                                                           ServiceNow notification (automated)

  **Tier 2 --- Medium**   CISO → CTO (Lauren Hessler)   CISO notified immediately upon classification; CTO notified within 4 hours of IRT activation   Phone and/or email

  **Tier 3 --- High**     CISO → CTO (Lauren Hessler)   CISO notified immediately upon classification; CTO notified within 2 hours of IRT activation   Phone call (primary); email confirmation to follow
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The escalation matrix above defines the mandatory notification chain for cybersecurity incidents addressed under this Plan. The escalation thresholds are designed to ensure that the CTO, as the senior technology executive and the approving authority for this Plan, is promptly informed of significant and critical incidents and is positioned to provide strategic guidance and resource support to the IRT.

For Tier 1 incidents, notification to the CISO is provided through automated ServiceNow alerts and post-resolution review, as the SOC team may handle Tier 1 incidents without full IRT activation. For Tier 2 and Tier 3 incidents, the CISO is notified immediately and serves as the Incident Commander, with subsequent escalation to the CTO within the timeframes specified above.

The Incident Commander shall document all escalation notifications in the ServiceNow incident record, including the date and time of each notification, the individual notified, the method of communication, and a summary of the information conveyed.

**[10.2 Escalation Procedures]{.underline}**

Upon classifying an incident as Tier 2 or Tier 3, the Incident Commander shall immediately activate the IRT and commence escalation in accordance with the matrix above. The Incident Commander shall provide the CTO with an initial briefing that includes: (a) the nature of the incident, (b) the preliminary severity classification, (c) the affected systems and estimated scope, (d) the containment actions taken or planned, and (e) the estimated timeline for resolution.

Escalation notifications shall be documented in the ServiceNow incident record. The Incident Commander shall provide ongoing status updates to the CTO at intervals determined by the severity of the incident --- at minimum every four (4) hours for Tier 3 incidents and every twelve (12) hours for Tier 2 incidents --- until the incident is resolved.

The CTO may, at her discretion, further escalate to additional Company leadership as she deems appropriate based on the nature and severity of the incident and its potential impact on business operations. Any such further escalation is at the CTO\'s sole discretion, and this Plan does not prescribe specific triggers, recipients, or timelines for escalation beyond the CTO.

**[APPENDICES]{.underline}**

 

**[Appendix A --- Definitions and Acronyms]{.underline}**

**[A.1 Definitions]{.underline}**

The following definitions apply to terms used throughout this Plan:

**Containment.** Actions taken to limit the spread and impact of a cybersecurity incident, including isolation of affected systems, revocation of compromised credentials, and implementation of emergency network controls.

**Critical System.** Any system classified as essential to core business operations, including ERP modules for finance, procurement, and order management, email infrastructure, and any other system whose extended unavailability would materially impair the Company\'s ability to conduct normal business operations.

**Cybersecurity Incident.** Any event that actually or potentially jeopardizes the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the Company\'s information systems or data, or that constitutes a violation of the Company\'s information security policies. See Section 3.1 for the complete definition.

**Endpoint.** Any computing device connected to the Company\'s network, including desktop workstations, laptops, servers, mobile devices, and IoT devices enrolled in the Company\'s endpoint management platform.

**Eradication.** Actions taken to completely remove the threat actor\'s presence, tools, malware, and access from the Company\'s IT environment following containment.

**Forensic Image.** A bit-for-bit copy of a storage device (hard drive, solid-state drive, or other storage media) created using industry-standard forensic imaging tools for purposes of subsequent technical analysis without altering the original evidence.

**Incident Commander.** The individual designated to lead and coordinate the Company\'s response to a cybersecurity incident. Under this Plan, the CISO serves as the Incident Commander. See Section 2.2.

**Incident Response Team (IRT).** The standing cross-functional team responsible for executing the Company\'s response to cybersecurity incidents under the direction of the Incident Commander. See Section 2.3.

**Recovery.** Actions taken to restore affected systems to normal operational status following eradication of the threat, including backup restoration, system rebuilds, data integrity validation, and staged reintroduction to the production network.

**ServiceNow.** The Company\'s IT service management platform used for incident ticketing, documentation, workflow management, and tracking of all incident-related actions and communications.

**SOC (Security Operations Center).** The Company\'s centralized security monitoring function, operating 24/7, responsible for detecting, analyzing, and initially triaging potential cybersecurity incidents.

**[Appendix D --- Incident Documentation Template (ServiceNow)]{.underline}**

The following template defines the standard fields and data elements to be captured for every cybersecurity incident documented in the ServiceNow incident management platform. The template ensures consistent and comprehensive documentation across all incidents and supports post-incident analysis and continuous improvement.

All fields marked with an asterisk (\*) are mandatory and must be completed before the incident ticket can be advanced to the next lifecycle stage.

**Incident Identification**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Field**                           **Description**
  ----------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Incident ID\*                       Auto-generated unique identifier assigned by ServiceNow upon ticket creation.

  Date/Time Detected\*                The date and time the incident was first detected or reported, recorded in Eastern Time (ET).

  Detected By\*                       Name of the SOC analyst or individual who identified or received the initial report of the incident.

  Detection Source\*                  The channel through which the incident was detected (e.g., automated alert --- SIEM, automated alert --- EDR, user report, vendor notification, law enforcement notification, customer report).
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Classification and Scope**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Field**                           **Description**
  ----------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Preliminary Severity Level\*        Initial severity classification assigned per Section 3.2 (Tier 1 --- Low / Tier 2 --- Medium / Tier 3 --- High).

  Final Severity Level                Final severity classification upon incident closure, reflecting any reclassifications made during the response.

  Affected Systems\*                  List of all systems, servers, applications, and network segments affected by the incident.

  Affected Endpoints\*                Count of endpoints confirmed or suspected to be compromised.

  Estimated Downtime                  Estimated total duration of system downtime attributable to the incident, in hours.

  Data Compromise Suspected\*         Yes / No. Indicates whether data exfiltration, unauthorized access to data, or data corruption is suspected or confirmed.

  Description of Incident\*           Narrative description of the incident, including the nature of the attack, observed indicators of compromise, and initial assessment of impact.
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Response Actions**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Field**                           **Description**
  ----------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  IRT Activation\*                    Yes / No. Indicates whether the full IRT was activated. If yes, record the date and time of activation.

  Containment Actions Taken           Description of all short-term and long-term containment actions executed, with timestamps.

  Eradication Actions Taken           Description of all eradication actions executed, with timestamps and validation results.

  Recovery Actions Taken              Description of all recovery actions executed, including backup restoration, system rebuilds, and data integrity validation, with timestamps.

  Third-Party Vendors Engaged         Names of any third-party vendors engaged during the incident, with dates of engagement and scope of work.
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Closure**

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Field**                           **Description**
  ----------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Incident Commander Sign-Off\*       Name and confirmation of the Incident Commander authorizing incident closure, with date and time.

  Incident Closure Date/Time\*        The date and time the incident was declared resolved and the ticket was closed.

  Post-Incident Report Reference      Reference number or link to the post-incident report prepared in accordance with Section 8.1.
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Notes:**

All entries in the ServiceNow incident record are timestamped automatically by the platform and attributed to the logged-in user. IRT members shall make contemporaneous entries in ServiceNow throughout the incident lifecycle to maintain an accurate and complete record. Modifications to prior entries should be made through addendum notations rather than overwriting existing content, to preserve a complete audit trail of all actions and decisions.

The ServiceNow incident template is maintained by the Information Security department and may be updated periodically to reflect changes in this Plan or the Company\'s operational requirements.
